title: The Slump In Asian Currencies
gist: The yen has fallen to ¥162.40/USD, its weakest since December 1986, and other Asian currencies (won, baht, rupiah) have also slumped, because dollar strength from expected Fed rate hikes is overwhelming the relief that a Gulf peace deal was supposed to bring to Asian oil-importer currencies.

insights.claim: The Japanese yen hit ¥162.40 against the US dollar in Asian trading on Tuesday, its weakest level since December 1986, in the early days of the bubble economy that followed the Plaza Accord.
insights.speaker: Udith Sikand, Tom Miller
insights.so_what: A yen at a 40-year low signals the current dollar-strength regime is now more extreme than at any point since the pre-bubble-economy era, a level worth watching for BoJ intervention risk or policy response.
insights.quote: the Japanese yen hit ¥162.40 against the US dollar, its weakest since December 1986, in the early days of the bubble economy following the signing of the Plaza Accord
insights.locator: Asian trading on Tuesday saw the Japanese yen

insights.claim: The yen's slide is part of a broader regional move, with the Korean won, Thai baht and Indonesian rupiah also falling steeply in recent weeks.
insights.speaker: Udith Sikand, Tom Miller
insights.so_what: The weakness is region-wide rather than yen-specific, pointing to a common dollar-strength driver rather than idiosyncratic Japan risk.
insights.quote: This latest drop in the yen follows steep falls in recent weeks from other Asian currencies including the Korean won, Thai baht and Indonesian rupiah.
insights.locator: This latest drop in the yen follows

themes.theme: Middle East peace dividend failed to materialize for Asian FX

insights.claim: Steps toward Middle East peace and a resumption of normal energy trade had been expected to ease the growth and currency pressure on Asian oil and gas importers, but the anticipated relief has not shown up in currency markets.
insights.speaker: Udith Sikand, Tom Miller
insights.so_what: The failure of the expected peace dividend to lift Asian currencies suggests investors should not assume geopolitical de-escalation alone will support EM Asia FX while the dollar is rallying.
insights.quote: It was not supposed to be like this. Steps towards peace in the Middle East and the beginning of a resumption in normal energy trade had been expected to bring relief for the currencies of Asian oil and gas importers.
insights.locator: It was not supposed to be like this

insights.claim: A preliminary Gulf deal was expected to reverse the downward revision in the region's growth outlook triggered by the war and to draw capital flows back into Asia.
insights.speaker: Udith Sikand, Tom Miller
insights.so_what: This was the consensus bull case for Asian assets post-ceasefire; its failure to play out is itself a signal that other forces (Fed policy) now dominate the flow picture.
insights.quote: A preliminary deal in the Gulf, it was thought, would reverse the downward revision in the region’s growth outlook that followed the start of the war and see capital begin to flow back into the region.
insights.locator: A preliminary deal in the Gulf

insights.claim: Asian currencies initially popped when the US and Iran first announced their memorandum of understanding, but have since weakened almost across the board as the dollar rallied on expectations the Federal Reserve will hike rates.
insights.speaker: Udith Sikand, Tom Miller
insights.so_what: Fed rate-hike expectations are now the dominant driver overpowering a geopolitical de-escalation tailwind, implying Asian FX weakness is a macro (rates) story, not a geopolitical one, and won't reverse until Fed expectations shift.
insights.quote: following an initial pop when the US and Iran first announced their memorandum of understanding, almost all Asian currencies have weakened as the US dollar has rallied on expectations that the Federal Reserve will hike rates

insights.claim: Gavekal frames the current dollar rally as tied to a debate over whether the US is in a concentrated inflationary boom or simply making a bet on future Fed tightening, a question addressed in a companion piece the authors cross-reference.
insights.speaker: Udith Sikand, Tom Miller
insights.so_what: Investors tracking Asian FX should also watch the US inflation/growth data debate, since the resolution of that question (inflationary boom vs. Fed bet) will determine how long dollar strength and Asian currency weakness persist.
insights.locator: see A Concentrated Inflationary Boom Or A Bet On The Fed?
